question:
2. Let
\[
R_{1}=1, \quad R_{n+1}=1+n / R_{n}, \quad n \geq 1
\]

Show that for \( n \geq 1 \),
\[
\sqrt{n} \leq R_{n} \leq \sqrt{n}+1
\]
solution:
Solution. We shall use induction on \( n \). Evidently \( \sqrt{1}=1=R_{1} \leq \sqrt{1}+1 \), so the given formula is true for \( n=1 \). Suppose we know that it is true for \( n=k \), i.e., for \( k \) a positive integer
\[
\sqrt{k} \leq R_{k} \leq \sqrt{k}+1
\]

Then
\[
\sqrt{k+1}-1=\frac{k}{\sqrt{k+1}+1}<\frac{k}{\sqrt{k}+1} \leq \frac{k}{R_{k}} \leq \frac{k}{\sqrt{k}}=\sqrt{k}<\sqrt{k+1} .
\]

Hence
\[
\sqrt{k+1}<1+\frac{k}{R_{k}}=R_{k+1}<\sqrt{k+1}+1
\]
and the induction is complete.
Note that the proof shows that the inequalities are strict for all integers, \( n>1 \).

Remark. This problem was considered by Leo Moser and Max Wyman, "On Solutions of \( x^{d}=1 \) in Symmetric Groups," Canadian Journal of Mathematics. vol. 7 (1955), pages 159-168.

They obtained the continued fraction expansion
\[
R_{n+1}=1+\frac{n}{1+} \frac{n-1}{1+} \frac{n-2}{1+} \ldots \frac{1}{1}
\]
which follows directly from the recurrence, \( R_{n+1}=1+n / R_{n} \) and proved that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(R_{n}-\sqrt{n}\right)=1 / 2 .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
2. Let
\[
seqfirst=1, \quad seqnext=1+indexvar / seqcurrent, \quad indexvar \geq 1
\]

Show that for \( indexvar \geq 1 \),
\[
\sqrt{indexvar} \leq seqcurrent \leq \sqrt{indexvar}+1
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We shall use induction on \( indexvar \). Evidently \( \sqrt{1}=1=seqfirst \leq \sqrt{1}+1 \), so the given formula is true for \( indexvar=1 \). Suppose we know that it is true for \( indexvar=indextemp \), i.e., for \( indextemp \) a positive integer
\[
\sqrt{indextemp} \leq seqiter \leq \sqrt{indextemp}+1
\]

Then
\[
\sqrt{indextemp+1}-1=\frac{indextemp}{\sqrt{indextemp+1}+1}<\frac{indextemp}{\sqrt{indextemp}+1} \leq \frac{indextemp}{seqiter} \leq \frac{indextemp}{\sqrt{indextemp}}=\sqrt{indextemp}<\sqrt{indextemp+1} .
\]

Hence
\[
\sqrt{indextemp+1}<1+\frac{indextemp}{seqiter}=seqnext<\sqrt{indextemp+1}+1
\]
and the induction is complete.
Note that the proof shows that the inequalities are strict for all integers, \( indexvar>1 \).

Remark. This problem was considered by Leo Moser and Max Wyman, "On Solutions of \( unknownx^{exponent}=1 \) in Symmetric Groups," Canadian Journal of Mathematics. vol. 7 (1955), pages 159-168.

They obtained the continued fraction expansion
\[
seqnext=1+\frac{indexvar}{1+} \frac{indexvar-1}{1+} \frac{indexvar-2}{1+} \ldots \frac{1}{1}
\]
which follows directly from the recurrence, \( seqnext=1+indexvar / seqcurrent \) and proved that
\[
\lim _{indexvar \rightarrow \infty}\left(seqcurrent-\sqrt{indexvar}\right)=1 / 2 .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. Let
\[
mandarins=1, \quad turntable=1+pinecone / rainstorm, \quad pinecone \geq 1
\]

Show that for \( pinecone \geq 1 \),
\[
\sqrt{pinecone} \leq rainstorm \leq \sqrt{pinecone}+1
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We shall use induction on \( pinecone \). Evidently \( \sqrt{1}=1=mandarins \leq \sqrt{1}+1 \), so the given formula is true for \( pinecone=1 \). Suppose we know that it is true for \( pinecone=chandelier \), i.e., for \( chandelier \) a positive integer
\[
\sqrt{chandelier} \leq backspace \leq \sqrt{chandelier}+1
\]

Then
\[
\sqrt{chandelier+1}-1=\frac{chandelier}{\sqrt{chandelier+1}+1}<\frac{chandelier}{\sqrt{chandelier}+1} \leq \frac{chandelier}{backspace} \leq \frac{chandelier}{\sqrt{chandelier}}=\sqrt{chandelier}<\sqrt{chandelier+1} .
\]

Hence
\[
\sqrt{chandelier+1}<1+\frac{chandelier}{backspace}=turntable<\sqrt{chandelier+1}+1
\]
and the induction is complete.
Note that the proof shows that the inequalities are strict for all integers, \( pinecone>1 \).

Remark. This problem was considered by Leo Moser and Max Wyman, "On Solutions of \( grainmill^{marshland}=1 \) in Symmetric Groups," Canadian Journal of Mathematics. vol. 7 (1955), pages 159-168.

They obtained the continued fraction expansion
\[
turntable=1+\frac{pinecone}{1+} \frac{pinecone-1}{1+} \frac{pinecone-2}{1+} \ldots \frac{1}{1}
\]
which follows directly from the recurrence, \( turntable=1+pinecone / rainstorm \) and proved that
\[
\lim _{pinecone \rightarrow \infty}\left(rainstorm-\sqrt{pinecone}\right)=1 / 2 .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. Let
\[
finalterm=1, \quad previousterm=1+zeroindex / outsideterm, \quad zeroindex \geq 1
\]

Show that for \( zeroindex \geq 1 \),
\[
\sqrt{zeroindex} \leq outsideterm \leq \sqrt{zeroindex}+1
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We shall use induction on \( zeroindex \). Evidently \( \sqrt{1}=1=finalterm \leq \sqrt{1}+1 \), so the given formula is true for \( zeroindex=1 \). Suppose we know that it is true for \( zeroindex=staticindex \), i.e., for \( staticindex \) a positive integer
\[
\sqrt{staticindex} \leq externalterm \leq \sqrt{staticindex}+1
\]

Then
\[
\sqrt{staticindex+1}-1=\frac{staticindex}{\sqrt{staticindex+1}+1}<\frac{staticindex}{\sqrt{staticindex}+1} \leq \frac{staticindex}{externalterm} \leq \frac{staticindex}{\sqrt{staticindex}}=\sqrt{staticindex}<\sqrt{staticindex+1} .
\]

Hence
\[
\sqrt{staticindex+1}<1+\frac{staticindex}{externalterm}=previousterm<\sqrt{staticindex+1}+1
\]
and the induction is complete.
Note that the proof shows that the inequalities are strict for all integers, \( zeroindex>1 \).

Remark. This problem was considered by Leo Moser and Max Wyman, "On Solutions of \( stillvalue^{baseexpo}=1 \) in Symmetric Groups," Canadian Journal of Mathematics. vol. 7 (1955), pages 159-168.

They obtained the continued fraction expansion
\[
previousterm=1+\frac{zeroindex}{1+} \frac{zeroindex-1}{1+} \frac{zeroindex-2}{1+} \ldots \frac{1}{1}
\]
which follows directly from the recurrence, \( previousterm=1+zeroindex / outsideterm \) and proved that
\[
\lim _{zeroindex \rightarrow \infty}\left(outsideterm-\sqrt{zeroindex}\right)=1 / 2 .
\]

garbled_string.question:
2. Let
\[
qzxwvtnp=1, \quad hjgrksla=1+pbrqkzvm / xldpftge, \quad pbrqkzvm \geq 1
\]

Show that for \( pbrqkzvm \geq 1 \),
\[
\sqrt{pbrqkzvm} \leq xldpftge \leq \sqrt{pbrqkzvm}+1
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We shall use induction on \( pbrqkzvm \). Evidently \( \sqrt{1}=1=qzxwvtnp \leq \sqrt{1}+1 \), so the given formula is true for \( pbrqkzvm=1 \). Suppose we know that it is true for \( pbrqkzvm=awfjchzu \), i.e., for \( awfjchzu \) a positive integer
\[
\sqrt{awfjchzu} \leq bmtrgqas \leq \sqrt{awfjchzu}+1
\]

Then
\[
\sqrt{awfjchzu+1}-1=\frac{awfjchzu}{\sqrt{awfjchzu+1}+1}<\frac{awfjchzu}{\sqrt{awfjchzu}+1} \leq \frac{awfjchzu}{bmtrgqas} \leq \frac{awfjchzu}{\sqrt{awfjchzu}}=\sqrt{awfjchzu}<\sqrt{awfjchzu+1} .
\]

Hence
\[
\sqrt{awfjchzu+1}<1+\frac{awfjchzu}{bmtrgqas}=hjgrksla<\sqrt{awfjchzu+1}+1
\]
and the induction is complete.
Note that the proof shows that the inequalities are strict for all integers, \( pbrqkzvm>1 \).

Remark. This problem was considered by Leo Moser and Max Wyman, "On Solutions of \( kvspqrol^{itxwznea}=1 \) in Symmetric Groups," Canadian Journal of Mathematics. vol. 7 (1955), pages 159-168.

They obtained the continued fraction expansion
\[
hjgrksla=1+\frac{pbrqkzvm}{1+} \frac{pbrqkzvm-1}{1+} \frac{pbrqkzvm-2}{1+} \ldots \frac{1}{1}
\]
which follows directly from the recurrence, \( hjgrksla=1+pbrqkzvm / xldpftge \) and proved that
\[
\lim _{pbrqkzvm \rightarrow \infty}\left(xldpftge-\sqrt{pbrqkzvm}\right)=1 / 2 .
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $a,b$ be strictly positive real numbers such that  
\[
\boxed{\;a\;\ge\;\sqrt{b}>0\;}
\tag{H}
\]

For $n\ge 1$ define the sequence $\bigl(X_{n}\bigr)$ recursively by  
\[
\boxed{\;X_{1}=a,\qquad 
        X_{\,n+1}=a+\dfrac{bn}{X_{n}}\;(n\ge 1)\;}
\tag{\mathcal R}
\]

1.  (Two-sided first-order squeeze) Show that, for every $n\ge 1$,
\[
\boxed{\;
      \sqrt{bn}\;\le\;X_{n}\;\le\;\sqrt{bn}+a\;}
\tag{\mathcal S}
\]

2.  (Exact first-order asymptotics) Put  
\[
D_{n}:=X_{n}-\sqrt{bn},\qquad 
E_{n}:=D_{n}-\dfrac a2 ,\qquad n\ge 1 .
\]
Prove that  
\[
\boxed{\;
       \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}D_{n}=\frac a2\;}
\tag{\mathcal L}
\]

3.  (Sharp second-order estimates)

   (a) Prove that there exists a constant $C(a,b)$ such that for all $n\ge 2$
   \[
   \boxed{\;
         \Bigl|X_{n}-\sqrt{bn}-\tfrac a2\Bigr|
         \le\frac{C(a,b)}{\sqrt n}\;}
   \tag{$\star$}
   \]

   (b) Show that if $a^{2}\neq 2b$ the exponent $-\tfrac12$ in $(\star)$
   is optimal (that is, no bound of the form $Cn^{-\alpha}$ with
   $\alpha>\tfrac12$ can hold for every $n$).

   (c) Treat the resonant case $a^{2}=2b$: prove that there exists
   $C^{\ast}(a,b)$ with  
   \[
         \bigl|X_{n}-\sqrt{bn}-\tfrac a2\bigr|
         \le\frac{C^{\ast}(a,b)}{n}\qquad(n\ge 2),
   \tag{$\star\star$}
   \]
   and that here the exponent $-1$ is best possible.

Only elementary real analysis (limits, inequalities, the mean-value
theorem, elementary series estimates) may be used.  



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we use  
\[
r_{n}:=\sqrt{bn},\qquad  
D_{n}:=X_{n}-r_{n},\qquad  
E_{n}:=D_{n}-\frac a2,\qquad n\ge 1 .
\tag{0.1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Proof of the squeeze $(\mathcal S)$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Exactly as in the original inductive proof one shows that every time
$r_{n}\le X_{n}\le r_{n}+a$ implies
$r_{n+1}\le X_{n+1}\le r_{n+1}+a$, whence $(\mathcal S)$ follows.
Consequently
\[
0\le D_{n}\le a,\qquad |E_{n}|\le a,\qquad n\ge 1 .
\tag{1.1}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  First-order asymptotics $(\mathcal L)$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  A convenient recursion.  
Because $r_{n}^{2}=bn$ we have
\[
X_{n+1}=a+\frac{bn}{X_{n}}
        =r_{n}+a-D_{n}+\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}},
\]
hence
\[
D_{n+1}=a-D_{n}+\varepsilon_{n},\qquad
\varepsilon_{n}:=\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}-\delta_{n},\qquad
\delta_{n}:=r_{n+1}-r_{n}.
\tag{2.1}
\]
Subtracting $a/2$ gives the alternating recursion
\[
E_{n+1}=-E_{n}+\varepsilon_{n}\qquad(n\ge 1).
\tag{2.2}
\]

2.2  A \emph{precise} expansion of $\varepsilon_{n}$.  
Write $D_{n}=a/2+E_{n}$.  Since $X_{n}=r_{n}+D_{n}$,
\[
\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}
     =\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{r_{n}}
        \Bigl(1+\frac{D_{n}}{r_{n}}\Bigr)^{-1}
     =\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{r_{n}}
      -\frac{D_{n}^{3}}{r_{n}^{2}}
      +O\!\bigl(n^{-3/2}\bigr)
      \quad(n\to\infty)
\tag{2.3a}
\]
(the remainder uses the boundedness of $D_{n}$, see (1.1)).
Because $r_{n}=\sqrt{b}\,n^{1/2}$,
\[
\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{r_{n}}
   =\frac{1}{\sqrt b\,\sqrt n}\Bigl(\frac{a^{2}}{4}+aE_{n}+E_{n}^{2}\Bigr),
\qquad
\frac{D_{n}^{3}}{r_{n}^{2}}
   =O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr).
\tag{2.3b}
\]
Furthermore
\[
\delta_{n}=r_{n+1}-r_{n}
          =\frac{\sqrt b}{2\sqrt n}-\frac{\sqrt b}{8\,n^{3/2}}
           +O\!\bigl(n^{-5/2}\bigr).
\tag{2.3c}
\]
Putting (2.3a)-(2.3c) together gives, with  
\[
c_{0}:=\frac{a^{2}-2b}{4\sqrt b},\qquad
c_{1}:=-\frac{a^{3}}{8b},
\]
the \emph{exact} formula
\[
\boxed{\;
\varepsilon_{n}
 =c_{0}\,n^{-1/2}
  +\frac{a}{\sqrt b}\,\frac{E_{n}}{\sqrt n}
  +\frac{E_{n}^{2}}{\sqrt b\,\sqrt n}
  +c_{1}\,n^{-1}
  +O\!\bigl(n^{-3/2}\bigr)\;}
\tag{2.4}
\]

2.3  The sequence $(E_{n})$ converges.  
Because of (1.1) every term on the right-hand side of (2.4) is
$O(n^{-1/2})$; hence $\varepsilon_{n}\to 0$.
From (2.2) we obtain
\[
E_{n+2}-E_{n}
      =\varepsilon_{n+1}-\varepsilon_{n}\longrightarrow 0 ,
\tag{2.5}
\]
so the even and odd subsequences are Cauchy and therefore
convergent.  Let $L:=\lim_{n\to\infty}E_{n}$.  Passing to the limit in
$E_{n+1}=-E_{n}+\varepsilon_{n}$ yields $L=-L$, hence $L=0$.
Therefore
\[
\lim_{n\to\infty}D_{n}=\frac a2 ,
\]
establishing $(\mathcal L)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Sharp second-order estimates
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Throughout we retain  
\[
c_{0}=\frac{a^{2}-2b}{4\sqrt b},\qquad
c_{1}=-\frac{a^{3}}{8b}.
\tag{3.0}
\]

3.1  A uniform bootstrap bound for $E_{n}$.  
Fix $K\!>\!2\bigl(|c_{0}|+a+1\bigr)$.  
We first show that
\[
|E_{n}|\le\frac{K}{\sqrt n}\qquad(n\text{ large})
\tag{3.1}
\]
by contradiction.  Suppose $|E_{N}|>\dfrac{K}{\sqrt N}$ for the {\it
first} index $N$.  Using (2.2) and (2.4) we write
\[
E_{N+1}=-E_{N}+c_{0}N^{-1/2}+R_{N},\qquad
|R_{N}|\le\Bigl(\frac{a}{\sqrt b}+a\Bigr)N^{-1/2}+C N^{-1},
\]
whence
\[
|E_{N+1}|
 \;\ge\;
|E_{N}|-|c_{0}|N^{-1/2}-|R_{N}|
 \;>\;
\frac{K-2|c_{0}|-2a}{\sqrt N}
 \;>\;
\frac{K}{\sqrt{N+1}}
\]
(for $N$ large enough).  This contradicts the minimality of $N$,
proving (3.1).  Consequently every term containing $E_{n}$ in
(2.4) is in fact $O(n^{-1})$ once $n$ is large.

3.2  The \emph{generic} case $a^{2}\neq 2b$ ($c_{0}\neq 0$).  
Because of (3.1), for large $n$
\[
\varepsilon_{n}=c_{0}n^{-1/2}+O(n^{-1}),
\quad\text{and hence}\quad
\varepsilon_{n+1}-\varepsilon_{n}
      =-\frac{c_{0}}{2}\,n^{-3/2}+O(n^{-3/2}).
\]
Summing $\varepsilon_{k+1}-\varepsilon_{k}$ from a fixed index up to
$n$ gives $\varepsilon_{n}=O(n^{-1/2})$, so by (2.5)
\[
E_{n+2}-E_{n}=O(n^{-1/2}),
\]
and a simple induction shows the existence of a constant
$C_{1}(a,b)$ with
\[
|E_{n}-\tfrac{c_{0}}{2}n^{-1/2}|\le\frac{C_{1}(a,b)}{n}\qquad(n\ge 2).
\]
Using again $|c_{0}|n^{-1/2}\le C_{1}/\sqrt n$ for the finitely many
small indices and going back to $D_{n}=a/2+E_{n}$ yields
\[
\boxed{\;
      \bigl|X_{n}-\sqrt{bn}-\tfrac a2\bigr|
      \le\frac{C(a,b)}{\sqrt n}\quad(n\ge 2)\;}
\]
which is $(\star)$.

\smallskip
\emph{Optimality.}  
Assume that, for some $\alpha>\tfrac12$, a bound
$|E_{n}|\le Cn^{-\alpha}$ held for all $n$.  Then
$\varepsilon_{n}=E_{n+1}+E_{n}=O(n^{-\alpha})$, contradicting
$\varepsilon_{n}\sim c_{0}n^{-1/2}$ with $c_{0}\neq 0$.  Hence the
exponent $-\tfrac12$ is best possible.

3.3  The \emph{resonant} case $a^{2}=2b$ ($c_{0}=0$).  
Now (2.4) simplifies to
\[
\varepsilon_{n}
      =\frac{a}{\sqrt b}\,\frac{E_{n}}{\sqrt n}
       +\frac{E_{n}^{2}}{\sqrt b\,\sqrt n}
       +c_{1}\,n^{-1}+O\!\bigl(n^{-3/2}\bigr).
\]
With the bootstrap bound (3.1) both terms containing $E_{n}$ are
$O(n^{-1})$, so $\varepsilon_{n}=c_{1}n^{-1}+O(n^{-3/2})$.  A
computation completely analogous to that in 3.2 gives, for a suitable
constant $C_{2}(a,b)$,
\[
|E_{n}-c_{1}n^{-1}|\le\frac{C_{2}(a,b)}{n^{3/2}}\qquad(n\ge 2),
\]
and therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
      \bigl|X_{n}-\sqrt{bn}-\tfrac a2\bigr|
      \le\frac{C^{\ast}(a,b)}{n}\quad(n\ge 2)\;}
\]
which is $(\star\star)$.

\smallskip
\emph{Optimality.}  
If a strictly better exponent $\alpha>1$ were attainable, then
$\varepsilon_{n}=E_{n+1}+E_{n}$ would be $O(n^{-\alpha})$, contradicting
$\varepsilon_{n}\sim c_{1}n^{-1}$ with $c_{1}\neq 0$.  Thus $-1$ is
sharp.

\hfill$\square$



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional Parameters:  Two free positive parameters a and b replace the fixed constants 1 and 3, enlarging the family of admissible recursions and removing scale-invariance.  
• Asymptotic Requirement:  Besides mere inequalities, Parts 2–3 demand evaluation of an exact limit and a quantitative convergence rate, forcing the solver to perform a delicate asymptotic expansion rather than straightforward induction.  
• Error Control:  Establishing the O(n^{-1/2}) remainder term and propagating it through the recursion introduces careful Taylor approximation, manipulation of error terms, and Cauchy-type convergence arguments.  
• Multiple Techniques:  The complete solution combines induction, algebraic manipulation of recurrences, Taylor series, estimation of alternating perturbations, and summability of error series—considerably deeper than the single-step comparison argument that suffices for the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we assume $(\mathrm H)$ and put  
\[
r_{n}:=\sqrt{bn},\quad  
D_{n}:=X_{n}-r_{n},\quad  
E_{n}:=D_{n}-\frac a2 ,\qquad n\ge 1 .
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
1.\;Proof of the squeeze $(\mathcal S)$  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
We argue by induction.  For $n=1$ the required inequalities are  
$\sqrt b\le a\le\sqrt b+a$, which follow from $(\mathrm H)$.  
Assume $\sqrt{bn}\le X_{n}\le\sqrt{bn}+a$ for some $n\ge 1$.  
Because $x\mapsto n/x$ is decreasing on $(0,\infty)$,  
\[
\frac{bn}{\sqrt{bn}+a} \;\le\;\frac{bn}{X_{n}}
\;\le\;\frac{bn}{\sqrt{bn}}=\sqrt{bn}.
\]
Adding $a$ shows
\[
\sqrt{bn} \;<\; a+\frac{bn}{X_{n}}
           \;<\; a+\sqrt{bn}
           =   \sqrt{b(n+1)}+(\underbrace{a-\bigl(\sqrt{b(n+1)}-\sqrt{bn}\bigr)}_{\ge 0}),
\]
hence
\[
\sqrt{b(n+1)}\;\le\;X_{n+1}\;\le\;\sqrt{b(n+1)}+a,
\]
completing the induction.

Two immediate corollaries will be used repeatedly:
\[
0\le D_{n}\le a,\qquad X_{n}\ge r_{n}\qquad(n\ge 1).
\tag{1.1}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
2.\;First-order asymptotics $(\mathcal L)$  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
Since $r_{n}^{2}=bn$,  
\[
X_{n+1}=a+\frac{bn}{X_{n}}
        =r_{n}+a-D_{n}+\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}.
\]
Hence  
\[
D_{n+1}=a-D_{n}+\varepsilon_{n},\qquad
\varepsilon_{n}:=\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}-\delta_{n},\qquad
\delta_{n}:=r_{n+1}-r_{n}.
\tag{2.1}
\]
Subtracting $\tfrac a2$ yields the linear recursion  
\[
E_{n+1}=-E_{n}+\varepsilon_{n}\qquad(n\ge 1).
\tag{2.2}
\]

Bound on $\varepsilon_{n}$.  
Using (1.1) and $\delta_{n}=\sqrt{b}/(\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt n)$,
\[
0\le\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}\le\frac{a^{2}}{r_{n}},\qquad
0<\delta_{n}\le\frac{\sqrt b}{2\sqrt n},
\]
whence
\[
|\varepsilon_{n}|
      \le\frac{a^{2}}{\sqrt{bn}}+\frac{\sqrt b}{2\sqrt n}
      =:\frac{M(a,b)}{\sqrt n}.
\tag{2.3}
\]

Cauchy convergence of $(E_{n})$.  
Iterating (2.2) gives  
\[
E_{n+1}+E_{n}=\varepsilon_{n},\qquad
E_{m+1}-(-1)^{\,m-n}E_{n+1}
          =\sum_{k=n+1}^{m}(-1)^{m-k}\varepsilon_{k}\quad(m>n).
\]
Because $(\varepsilon_{k})$ is bounded by $M(a,b)k^{-1/2}$ the right-hand
sum forms a Cauchy sequence; hence $(E_{n})_{n\ge 1}$ is Cauchy and
therefore convergent.  
But the relation $E_{n+1}=-E_{n}+\varepsilon_{n}\to 0$ forces the common
limit to be $0$.  Consequently $D_{n}=a/2+E_{n}\to a/2$, proving
$(\mathcal L)$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  
3.\;Second-order estimates  
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%  

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.1.\;An exact representation for $E_{n}$  
--------------------------------------------------------------------  
Iterating (2.2) and writing $\sigma_{k}:=(-1)^{\,k}$ we obtain the **exact**
identity
\[
E_{n}=(-1)^{\,n-1}E_{1}+\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(-1)^{\,n-1-k}\varepsilon_{k},
\qquad n\ge 2,
\tag{3.1}
\]
with
\[
E_{1}=D_{1}-\frac a2=\frac a2-\sqrt b.
\tag{3.2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.2.\;Precise expansion of $\varepsilon_{n}$  
--------------------------------------------------------------------  
Write $D_{n}=a/2+E_{n}$ and $X_{n}=r_{n}+D_{n}$.  
A standard Taylor expansion (details below) gives  
\[
\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}
     =\frac{a^{2}}{4r_{n}}
       +\frac{(a^{2}-2b)E_{n}}{4br_{n}}
       +O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr),
\]
so that
\[
\varepsilon_{n}
  =\frac{a^{2}-2b}{4\sqrt b}\,n^{-1/2}
   +\frac{\sqrt b}{8}\,n^{-3/2}
   +O\!\bigl(n^{-5/2}\bigr).
\tag{3.3}
\]
(The numerical constants come from the first three terms of the binomial
series for $\sqrt{n+1}-\sqrt n$ and from cancelling the contribution of
$E_{n}$, see the end of this subsection.)  Denote  
\[
c_{0}:=\frac{a^{2}-2b}{4\sqrt b},\qquad
c_{1}:=\frac{\sqrt b}{8}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.3.\;Eventual monotonicity of $|\varepsilon_{n}|$  
--------------------------------------------------------------------  
When $c_{0}\neq 0$, the dominant term $c_{0}n^{-1/2}$ clearly makes
$|\varepsilon_{n}|$ ultimately decrease.  
If $c_{0}=0$ (that is, $a^{2}=2b$) the leading term is $c_{1}n^{-3/2}$,
again decreasing.  Consequently there exists $N_{0}=N_{0}(a,b)$ such that  
\[
|\varepsilon_{n+1}|\le|\varepsilon_{n}|\qquad(n\ge N_{0}).
\tag{3.4}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.4.\;Proof of the {\bf upper} bounds $(\star)$ and $(\star\star)$  
--------------------------------------------------------------------  
Introduce  
\[
F_{n}:=(-1)^{\,n-1}E_{n}=E_{1}+\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(-1)^{k}\varepsilon_{k},
\qquad n\ge 2.
\tag{3.5}
\]
Because $|\varepsilon_{k}|$ is decreasing after $N_{0}$ and converges to
$0$, the alternating series
$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{k}\varepsilon_{k}$ converges (Leibniz criterion)
and for $n\ge N_{0}$
\[
|E_{n}|=|F_{n}|
       \le|\varepsilon_{n}|
       \le\frac{|c_{0}|+1}{\sqrt n}\qquad
       (a^{2}\neq 2b),
\]
respectively
\[
|E_{n}|=|F_{n}|
       \le|\varepsilon_{n}|
       \le\frac{|c_{1}|+1}{n^{3/2}}\qquad
       (a^{2}=2b).
\]
Enlarging the constant finitely many times if necessary gives $(\star)$
and $(\star\star)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3.5.\;Optimality of the exponents  
--------------------------------------------------------------------  

\noindent\emph{(i)  The case $a^{2}\neq 2b$.}  
Suppose that for some $\alpha>\tfrac12$ one had
$|E_{n}|\le Cn^{-\alpha}$ for every $n\ge 2$.  
Then $|\varepsilon_{n}|=|E_{n+1}+E_{n}|
                      \le C\bigl((n+1)^{-\alpha}+n^{-\alpha}\bigr)
                      \le 2C\,n^{-\alpha}$.  
But (3.3) shows
$\varepsilon_{n}=c_{0}n^{-1/2}+o\!\bigl(n^{-1/2}\bigr)$ with $c_{0}\neq 0$,
contradicting $\alpha>\tfrac12$.  Hence the exponent $-\tfrac12$ is sharp.

\smallskip
\noindent\emph{(ii)  The resonant case $a^{2}=2b$.}  
Assume that a bound $|E_{n}|\le Cn^{-\alpha}$ holds for all $n$ with
some $\alpha>\tfrac32$.  As above this gives
$|\varepsilon_{n}|\le 2C\,n^{-\alpha}$, while (3.3) now reads
$\varepsilon_{n}=c_{1}n^{-3/2}+o\!\bigl(n^{-3/2}\bigr)$ with the
\emph{non-zero} constant $c_{1}=\sqrt b/8$.  This is impossible if
$\alpha>\tfrac32$.  Therefore the exponent $-\tfrac32$ in $(\star\star)$
is optimal.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Justification of expansion (3.3).}  
Put $h_{n}:=D_{n}/r_{n}=a/(2r_{n})+E_{n}/r_{n}$.  
Since $r_{n+1}-r_{n}
      =\sqrt b/(\sqrt{n+1}+\sqrt n)
      =\sqrt b\,n^{-1/2}/2-\sqrt b\,n^{-3/2}/8+O(n^{-5/2})$,
the difference
\[
\varepsilon_{n}
   =\frac{D_{n}^{2}}{X_{n}}-\bigl(r_{n+1}-r_{n}\bigr)
   =\frac{r_{n}h_{n}^{2}}{1+h_{n}}
    -\frac{\sqrt b}{2}n^{-1/2}
    +\frac{\sqrt b}{8}n^{-3/2}+O(n^{-5/2})
\]
can be expanded with the help of
$r_{n}h_{n}^{2}=r_{n}(a^{2}/4r_{n}^{2}+O(n^{-1}))=
(a^{2}/4\sqrt b)\,n^{-1/2}+O(n^{-1})$
and $(1+h_{n})^{-1}=1-h_{n}+O(n^{-1})$.  Collecting all terms of order
$n^{-1/2}$ and $n^{-3/2}$ produces (3.3).

\hfill$\square$  

--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Additional Parameters:  Two free positive parameters a and b replace the fixed constants 1 and 3, enlarging the family of admissible recursions and removing scale-invariance.  
• Asymptotic Requirement:  Besides mere inequalities, Parts 2–3 demand evaluation of an exact limit and a quantitative convergence rate, forcing the solver to perform a delicate asymptotic expansion rather than straightforward induction.  
• Error Control:  Establishing the O(n^{-1/2}) remainder term and propagating it through the recursion introduces careful Taylor approximation, manipulation of error terms, and Cauchy-type convergence arguments.  
• Multiple Techniques:  The complete solution combines induction, algebraic manipulation of recurrences, Taylor series, estimation of alternating perturbations, and summability of error series—considerably deeper than the single-step comparison argument that suffices for the original problem.
